High-level seems reasonable (haven't reviewed tests yet).

I do wonder a bit whether a callback API is really what we want here; the disadvantage is that (as `add_done_callback()` explains) the error handling story is pretty bad in the event that some exception happens within the handling logic itself (which includes the callback provided by the caller of this utility):

> Exceptions raised in the callback will be logged at ERROR level, but will not terminate any threads of execution.

So I guess depending on how you plan to structure the calling code, this seems like it could be an issue that either causes the uploader to hang forever (if the calling thread waits on the completion callback to flip some success flag) or causes it to drop data at upload time "quietly", aka with only an error in the logs rather than actually aborting (if we fire-and-forget the write RPC and basically don't check if the completion callback is ever called).

A possible compromise is to have the calling thread wait on completion with a timeout that's greater than the maximum of each RPC wait time plus the total possible backoff, but it's a little ugly to have to essentially "know" how long the retrying logic might run in order to wait for the right length of time.

The alternative API that resolves this would be making `async_call_with_retries()` return a future that the calling thread can hold onto and wait on confidently (knowing it'll unblock eventually).  That future would essentially be a proxy over the future from the last attempt at sending the async RPC; blocking on the proxy future effectively just turns `async_call_with_retries` into `call_with_retries`.  In that case, we can still set `add_done_callback()` on the underlying future to use as a hint to basically trigger a preemptive retry in the event of a retryable failure, without waiting for the calling thread to block on our completion, but we're protected in the event that the callback doesn't exit cleanly.
Would love for @annatisch to take a look, but for such a small API, we can be a lot more concise and consistent.

Thanks for opening this RFC!

There are a few high-level things that I'm concerned about:
1. The `with_column()` column adding/mutating API isn't vectorized and is a pretty thin layer over `map_batches()`, so it's sure to be much slower than the `map_batches()` equivalent without a large improvement in UX.
2. The fact that each of these add/mutate/select/drop operations results in a separate `map_batches()` execution stage will make it much less efficient than the single `map_batches()` alternative.

For a concrete before/after comparison, here's the preprocessing stages from the big data training CUJ, along with an added bit to drop a column.

## Before RFC

```python
        def batch_transformer(df: pd.DataFrame):
            # Drop columns.
            df = df.drop(columns["bad_col_1", "bad_col_2"])

            # Drop nulls.
            df = df.dropna(subset=["nullable_feature"])

            # Add new column.
            df["new_col"] = (
                df["feature_1"] - 2 * df["feature_2"] + df["feature_3"]
            ) / 3.0

            # Transform column.
            df["feature_1"] = 2.0 * df["feature_1"] + 0.1

            return df

        ds = ds.map_batches(batch_transformer, batch_format="pandas")
```

## After RFC

```python
        ds = ds.drop_columns("bad_col_1", "bad_col_2") \
            .with_column(
                "new_col", lambda r: (r["feature_1"] - 2 * r["feature_2"] + r["feature_3"]) / 3.0) \
            .with_column("feature_1", lambda r: 2.0 * r["feature_1"] + 1) \
            .map_batches(lambda df: df.dropna(subset=["nullable_feature"]), batch_format="pandas")
```

I don't think that the latter is a big improvement in UX, and it will probably end up being a good bit slower. 🤔 

What do you think?

My only suggestion is that I think we should keep it together with our other config and environment handling rather than in a separate part of the codebase.

The current ones are in [LocalConfig](https://github.com/opendatacube/datacube-core/blob/3f9a7fe8950dad8f057d6ebda6b3c1a025e2c96e/datacube/config.py#L60-L62), and all fields there can be defined in one conf file and kept together. And the other envionment variables are handled in that class too.

(See [DATACUBE_ENVIRONMENT](https://github.com/opendatacube/datacube-core/blob/91e0f0b78fa7aec2947c3cd6a12e5716edbb4dfa/datacube/config.py#L87), and [DATACUBE_DB_URL](https://github.com/opendatacube/datacube-core/blob/91e0f0b78fa7aec2947c3cd6a12e5716edbb4dfa/datacube/config.py#L195) as examples of existing env vars -- both of which override fields that exist in the original config)

Minor things:
- Prefer `DATACUBE_` prefix instead of `ODC_` for consistency with other env variables. Maybe `DATACUBE_IAM_AUTHENTICATION`, as in theory a future index may not be RDS (?).
- Ideally make that auth token refresh time a config option too while we're here -- it feels inevitable that different environments will want to customise it (?)

First of all, so exciting to see you back around here @dmontagu! 🤩🎉

## PR Review

Now to the PR review:

I've been reviewing this for the last couple of days. Very clever all around. Amazing job, as always! 🚀

I added a couple of comments here and there. A couple of opinions in the places where I suspect my opinion could be relevant.

## Type Checks from VS Code - PyRight

I noticed a couple of errors with VS Code/PyRight in some places, I think mainly about getting a dict key form a dict where it is not required, or things like that.

Would it be valuable to point them out and suggest the fixes or is it just too much noise on top of the actual PR review?

I'm just adding a couple of those comments for now.

## Questions

About some of the questions in the description, my opinions inline.

Pretty much in favor with everything, for the rest I don't have a strong opinion or enough knowledge.

**Things left to resolve:**

* [ ] Field kwargs
	* [x] Should we remove or keep the 'examples' kwarg on `Field`?
		* If not, what is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Re-add the examples keyword argument
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. All things equal, +1 to re-adding it. Unless there's something against it.
	* [x] Should we remove or keep the 'const' kwarg on `Field`? (This was used to indicate a field was required and the only allowed value was its default.)
		* If not, what is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop this argument in favor of `Literal` and/or validators as appropriate (i.e., keep as is on main branch)
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. If it's the same, I would think it's better to keep one single way to do it, and if there's now a "standard" way to do it with type annotations, I would use that. One single way reduces combinatorial logic, maintenance, testing. And it's easier for users to learn one single thing than two, even more if it's two competing ones.
	* [ ] Should we change the `regex` kwarg to `pattern` in the `Field` signature?
		* **Tentative plan:** Change `regex` to `pattern` (i.e., keep as is on main branch)
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. As JSON Schema uses the same `pattern` keyword, I would vote for that. `regex` might be more intuitive from Python-land, but for me, `regex` is more ambiguous as to if it takes a regular expression object or a pattern string. Even though the Python docs also refer to a Pattern object, but anyway, I personally associate the word "pattern" more easily with the string.
	* [ ] Do we want to do a backwards-compatibility handler thing with `**kwargs` — warning about now-invalid kwargs (whether or not we keep them working) on `Field`?
		* **Tentative plan:** Do not attempt to maintain backwards compatibility, but document in the migration guide
			* [@tiangolo] **Strongly** in favor with plan. More details below, and also inline on the PR.


* [ ] ConfigDict fields
	* [ ] Do we want to re-add 'schema_extra' as a field on model_config?
		* If not, should we drop the tests?
		* Either way, do we retain/drop support on a config _class_ in the model definition?
		* What is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop in favor of `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan.
	* [ ] Do we want to re-add 'fields' as a field on model_config?
		* If not, should we drop the tests?
		* What is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop in favor of annotations and/or `= Field(...)`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan.

* [ ] Default values
	* If a default value of a field is not JSON encodable, do we include anything in the schema indicating the default value? Do we error?
	* **Tentative plan:** Just don't include the value in the schema if json encoding fails, but keep the `required` field set appropriately. I believe this is perfectly compatible with JSON schema.
		* [@tiangolo] Soft in favor with plan. I'm not sure about the implications, but I think it should be fine.

* [ ] `__modify_schema__` (-> `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`)
	* Do we retain backwards compatibility with the name `__modify_schema__`, or force migration to `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`?
		* **Tentative plan:** Force the migration to `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan. This is the moment to break changes.
	* Do we support any args/kwargs for `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__` besides the schema itself?
		* Do we pass any info to it from FieldInfo? The CoreSchema? A "source" object? (Could be useful when working with custom generics)
		* **Tentative plan:** Not sure. Would potentially be useful to have access to the core schema and to the type "source" (e.g., to check generic parameter values, etc.)

* [ ] FastAPI
	* Do we need to retain functions analogous to any of the following? (Note we have retained `model_schema`, and `schema`.)
		* get_flat_models_from_field/get_flat_models_from_fields
		* get_flat_models_from_model/get_flat_models_from_models
		* get_model_name_map
		* model_process_schema
	* If not, can we drop their tests?
	* **Tentative plan:** Could we instead build a CoreSchema in FastAPI that produces the desired JSON schema?
		* I'm inclined to put significant effort into helping with the migration of FastAPI to pydantic v2, and to implement any necessary analogous functions as a part of that effort. But if it's already clear that we will need them, happy to add them now.
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan. And thanks, as always! 🎉 Better not to add premature optimizations/abstractions. And more details in line in the PR.

* [ ] Callable type
	* How should we handle JSON schema generation for fields with type callable? (See, e.g., `tests.test_schema.test_callable_type`)
	* **Tentative plan:** Not sure.

* [ ] Decimal
	* What should the Decimal schema be?
	* **Tentative plan:** Need to come to a consensus; https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/1511 may be relevant
		* Note: although serialization and parsing are different, I think for FastAPI/OpenAPI purposes it is important that the JSON schema be compatible with the serialization type. (Or else generated clients will produce type errors if an API returns a Decimal.) Happy to discuss this more.
			* [@tiangolo] agreed that incompatible clients would be a non-obvious but important problem. Although I'm not sure I understand the problem well and what's the "serialization type" in this context.

## kwargs in `Field()`

Details about asking for no more kwargs in `Field()`:

I would request to **not** accept any extra kwargs. I asked for that here in this hidden comment: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/4516#discussion_r1010870226

Copying here for context/completeness:

> This `**extra` has been used to add stuff to JSON Schema.

> But in some cases, people pass other stuff here expecting it to be used later or for other purposes, but then it also affects JSON Schema.

> I would like to ask to make it a single keyword argument `extra` instead of `**extra`, or alternatively, to have another single argument `json_schema_extra` so that extensions for JSON Schema can be apart from extra values passed here for other purposes.

> I haven't seen a use for this apart from JSON Schema, and the fact that it makes `Field()` accept any keyword arguments makes editors not complain about arguments that actually do nothing. I've seen this sometimes, e.g. recently with a `Field(required=True)`, but `required` is not a valid argument, if it's required or not is defined by the absence of a `default`. But the code seemed fine and linters passed, because it was taken by the catch all `**extra`.

> It's a breaking change, but I think it's totally worth it and this is the right time for those changes.

## Semi-off topic, highlights

**Note**: no action item here, just praise and highlights of non-obvious extra benefits. 🎉

Very clever implementation! 🚀

I like how the core schema refs, def refs, and json refs are stored and correlated, and then updated when necessary, but still stored. And how everything is stored in the object.

I think this will allow me to do something I've been wanting to do in FastAPI, to pre-generate the JSON Schema stuff for each endpoint, and when there's an error, to show the specific endpoint with an error instead of just saying "something is wrong".

## Wishlist - collect errors

I was checking the wishlist of things I wanted for Pydantic and almost everything is already done. 🎉

From the things not there yet, there's one single thing I would like to ask that is related to JSON Schema, although not sure if it could be a subsequent PR. It's also related to the point above.

### The problem

First the problem.

Currently, an example like this, rightfully breaks:

```Pyhton
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict


class Car:
    def __init__(self, make: str, model: str, year: int):
        self.make = make
        self.model = model
        self.year = year

class Model(BaseModel):
    f1: int = 1
    f2: Car

    model_config = ConfigDict(arbitrary_types_allowed=True)


Model.model_json_schema()
```

But if `Model` is in a FastAPI path operation, and maybe `Car` is in a sub-model in a sub-field, in a large app, when calling the OpenAPI endpoint the whole thing will break, saying there's a problem with `Car`, but without saying where exactly... and more importantly, none of the OpenAPI will be generated. So, a single broken field breaks the whole OpenAPI and Swagger UI.

### Feature Request

Currently it immediately raises an error like:

```
Cannot generate a JsonSchema for X
```

...that stops the rest of the JSON Schema generation.

I would like instead to accumulate those errors and get them all together, but to be able to retrieve the rest of the JSON Schema.

### Possible Implementation

I think it's better to state the problem in case you see a better way to solve it, but I can also imagine possible implementations. For example:

```Python
schema = Model.model_json_schema()
```

...raises as normally, but:

```Python
schema, errors = Model.model_json_schema(keep_errors=True)
```

Doesn't raise, and returns a JSON Schema where anything with an error is replaced with `{}` and `errors` is a list of stored exceptions. Of course, `keep_errors` could use some bikeshedding on the parameter name, the same for the return interface, but you get the idea.

It could also be a context manager, maybe too complex, but that would keep the API interface (params, return) of `model_json_schema()` unmodified for both cases, don't know. And it would probably only be used by FastAPI and libraries like that, so not too bad on that side. But it would require contextvars and all that dance. E.g.

```Python
with ErrorCollector as c:
	schema = Model.model_json_schema()
print(e.errors)
```

Again, the specific implementation is up for discussion, but I would like to have a way to do this.

### Plan in FastAPI

My plan is also later to put this in FastAPI in a way that (if I manage to) when there's an error like this, the traceback would use Rich and point to the right file and line with the specific FastAPI *path operation* that uses that model. But I think that to be able to do that I first need to pre-compute and collect the JSON Schema errors per path-operation, and not only at the global level.

## Breaking Changes

About the breaking changes, mostly renaming things, in the small few places this happens, I think this is the right moment to do it and discard the cruft of backwards compatibility for those things.

An improvement in that migration, alternative to keep backwards compatibility, would be a tool to automatically update code to use the new things, for the simple ones (I can imagine, e.g. moving kwargs in `Field()` to `json_schema_extra`, or changing `regex` to `pattern`). Maybe that could also work for the `model_config`.

I haven't done it, but I know it's possible and feasible with LibCST. @Kludex has built a couple of CLI tools that do that type of thing, e.g. https://github.com/Kludex/bump-testclient

Hi @AllanOXDi - lots of new work here, which is really exciting to see! There is some smoothing out to be done on a few parts of the interface, as well as with some of the CRUD functionality of the device syncing schedule itself. To start with the syncing, I've added an inline comment about editing, but also wanted to add a little bit more context. When I click on the edit button, I do see the page is created and loads, but there are some console errors and no contents of the page. I'm not sure if this is the step you were at, or if the goal was to have the "edit" feature fully working. If you want to have the edit feature fully working, it seems like there are two errors happening, one related to your Vuex, and one related to the endpoint returning a 404 error. I'd suggest just debugging one at a time (perhaps with some route adjustments to make sure the device ID is also getting passed, it seems like that might be part of the problem), and then seeing if you can bring both pieces together. As for the UI, there is some general work that can be done to help it match the figma even more closely, even though it's just small things. First up: - overall, the page text looks a little bit "crowded" here. You can look at the figma and perhaps add a little bit of spacing. In some places, if you are using just `{{ $tr('here's my string') }}` to add text inside of a `div` or a `span`, wrapping it in a `

` tag can add some default padding that makes it feel a little bit roomier. You may still need to add a `class` to that `

` and adjust the spacing, but that might be a place to start. - You can see that the contents shifts when the dropdown is opened, and that it's a bit hard to scroll. I think adding a default, fixed height to the main page container, rather than just having it the size of the content that is there (for example, making it always take up ~80% of the page) might be a good idea. What do you think @jtamiace? https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/17235236/219707498-b84f2035-002d-4e1f-87d3-4b5b8b381339.mp4 - Finally, this is a bit of a nitpick, but to save Jessica having to do it, the alignment on the left should all be in one line. One tool I use to help with "pixel perfect" UIs (well, they are pixel perfect in my mind, although Jessica and Tomiwa might have a different opinion) is a Chrome browser extension called "Grid Rule". If you use Chrome you can just search for it and add it on. Turn on the extension, and then you can "pull" lines from the top or the left, to check the alignment of various page elements. https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/17235236/219709118-11e1f1bc-d3f8-45d8-b63c-ee2fbb480348.mp4 On the main sync schedules page, I have two suggestions. - Add some kind of loading state. One option would be to have a loading state for the whole table. The other option would be to have a loading state just for the sync status, if the other pieces of the table (i.e. device name) wouldn't take an extra fetch. This would help reduce the time where it looks like there are no devices - I'm not sure why there are four statuses here. I tried to figure it out from the code and it seems like `myDevices` is being set to `tasks` - if there is one task per device, that would be right, but here, it seems like there might be more than one task per device, in which case we may need to rethink how this is being displayed to the user. https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/17235236/219710099-7028e4ea-5454-4242-bb06-d4133da2714e.mp4 Finally - and this maybe is a question for @rtibbles regarding the expected behavior (maybe with @bjester weighing in if any of the refactoring work you are doing could relate to this?) I am now running into fun edge cases since I'm on ZeroTier and I can see other's devices available. This morning I saw both Samson's and Allan's computers in the "devices" that were on network. Trying to set a sync schedule on these (logically) resulted in a 403 error. Do we want to filter those devices out entirely? Do we want to filter them out for now, but in the future, add an option to input admin credentials in them, so that they could sync? Just wanted to raise this so we can start thinking about it. Okay - that's all I have for now, but let me know any questions you have or where you might need more tips or details, and I will do my best to help! Great progress on this!

If it's the same, I would think it's better to keep one single way to do it, and if there's now a "standard" way to do it with type annotations, I would use that. One single way reduces combinatorial logic, maintenance, testing. And it's easier for users to learn one single thing than two, even more if it's two competing ones.
	* [x] Should we change the `regex` kwarg to `pattern` in the `Field` signature?
		* **Tentative plan:** Change `regex` to `pattern` (i.e., keep as is on main branch)
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. As JSON Schema uses the same `pattern` keyword, I would vote for that. `regex` might be more intuitive from Python-land, but for me, `regex` is more ambiguous as to if it takes a regular expression object or a pattern string. Even though the Python docs also refer to a Pattern object, but anyway, I personally associate the word "pattern" more easily with the string.
	* [x] Do we want to do a backwards-compatibility handler thing with `**kwargs` — warning about now-invalid kwargs (whether or not we keep them working) on `Field`?
		* **Tentative plan:** Do not attempt to maintain backwards compatibility, but document in the migration guide
			* [@tiangolo] **Strongly** in favor with plan. More details below, and also inline on the PR.


* [x] ConfigDict fields
	* [x] Do we want to re-add 'schema_extra' as a field on model_config?
		* If not, should we drop the tests?
		* Either way, do we retain/drop support on a config _class_ in the model definition?
		* What is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop in favor of `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan.
	* [x] Do we want to re-add 'fields' as a field on model_config?
		* If not, should we drop the tests?
		* What is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop in favor of annotations and/or `= Field(...)`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan.

	* **Tentative plan:** Just don't include the value in the schema if json encoding fails, but keep the `required` field set appropriately. I believe this is perfectly compatible with JSON schema.
		* [@tiangolo] Soft in favor with plan. I'm not sure about the implications, but I think it should be fine.

## kwargs in `Field()`

Details about asking for no more kwargs in `Field()`:

I would request to **not** accept any extra kwargs. I asked for that here in this hidden comment: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/4516#discussion_r1010870226

Copying here for context/completeness:

> This `**extra` has been used to add stuff to JSON Schema.

> But in some cases, people pass other stuff here expecting it to be used later or for other purposes, but then it also affects JSON Schema.

> I would like to ask to make it a single keyword argument `extra` instead of `**extra`, or alternatively, to have another single argument `json_schema_extra` so that extensions for JSON Schema can be apart from extra values passed here for other purposes.

> I haven't seen a use for this apart from JSON Schema, and the fact that it makes `Field()` accept any keyword arguments makes editors not complain about arguments that actually do nothing. I've seen this sometimes, e.g. recently with a `Field(required=True)`, but `required` is not a valid argument, if it's required or not is defined by the absence of a `default`. But the code seemed fine and linters passed, because it was taken by the catch all `**extra`.

> It's a breaking change, but I think it's totally worth it and this is the right time for those changes.

## Semi-off topic, highlights

**Note**: no action item here, just praise and highlights of non-obvious extra benefits. 🎉

Very clever implementation! 🚀

I like how the core schema refs, def refs, and json refs are stored and correlated, and then updated when necessary, but still stored. And how everything is stored in the object.

I think this will allow me to do something I've been wanting to do in FastAPI, to pre-generate the JSON Schema stuff for each endpoint, and when there's an error, to show the specific endpoint with an error instead of just saying "something is wrong".

## Wishlist - collect errors

I was checking the wishlist of things I wanted for Pydantic and almost everything is already done. 🎉

From the things not there yet, there's one single thing I would like to ask that is related to JSON Schema, although not sure if it could be a subsequent PR. It's also related to the point above.

### The problem

First the problem.

Currently, an example like this, rightfully breaks:

```Pyhton
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict


class Car:
    def __init__(self, make: str, model: str, year: int):
        self.make = make
        self.model = model
        self.year = year

class Model(BaseModel):
    f1: int = 1
    f2: Car

    model_config = ConfigDict(arbitrary_types_allowed=True)


Model.model_json_schema()
```

But if `Model` is in a FastAPI path operation, and maybe `Car` is in a sub-model in a sub-field, in a large app, when calling the OpenAPI endpoint the whole thing will break, saying there's a problem with `Car`, but without saying where exactly... and more importantly, none of the OpenAPI will be generated. So, a single broken field breaks the whole OpenAPI and Swagger UI.

### Feature Request

Currently it immediately raises an error like:

```
Cannot generate a JsonSchema for X
```

...that stops the rest of the JSON Schema generation.

I would like instead to accumulate those errors and get them all together, but to be able to retrieve the rest of the JSON Schema.

### Possible Implementation

I think it's better to state the problem in case you see a better way to solve it, but I can also imagine possible implementations. For example:

```Python
schema = Model.model_json_schema()
```

...raises as normally, but:

```Python
schema, errors = Model.model_json_schema(keep_errors=True)
```

Doesn't raise, and returns a JSON Schema where anything with an error is replaced with `{}` and `errors` is a list of stored exceptions. Of course, `keep_errors` could use some bikeshedding on the parameter name, the same for the return interface, but you get the idea.

It could also be a context manager, maybe too complex, but that would keep the API interface (params, return) of `model_json_schema()` unmodified for both cases, don't know. And it would probably only be used by FastAPI and libraries like that, so not too bad on that side. But it would require contextvars and all that dance. E.g.

```Python
with ErrorCollector as c:
	schema = Model.model_json_schema()
print(e.errors)
```

Again, the specific implementation is up for discussion, but I would like to have a way to do this.

### Plan in FastAPI

My plan is also later to put this in FastAPI in a way that (if I manage to) when there's an error like this, the traceback would use Rich and point to the right file and line with the specific FastAPI *path operation* that uses that model. But I think that to be able to do that I first need to pre-compute and collect the JSON Schema errors per path-operation, and not only at the global level.

## Breaking Changes

About the breaking changes, mostly renaming things, in the small few places this happens, I think this is the right moment to do it and discard the cruft of backwards compatibility for those things.

An improvement in that migration, alternative to keep backwards compatibility, would be a tool to automatically update code to use the new things, for the simple ones (I can imagine, e.g. moving kwargs in `Field()` to `json_schema_extra`, or changing `regex` to `pattern`). Maybe that could also work for the `model_config`.

I haven't done it, but I know it's possible and feasible with LibCST. @Kludex has built a couple of CLI tools that do that type of thing, e.g. https://github.com/Kludex/bump-testclient
One important note about multiple inheritance and the super() call. the rest are nits.

To be extra careful, I think it's worth having one in the middle of the day or hour. I won't block this though.

Thanks for fixing btw.

For the test, I think a good test would be to generate an image that creates all possible flow values. Something like this:

```py
import torch
from torchvision.utils import flow_to_image
from torchvision.io import write_jpeg
h, w = 100, 100

flow = torch.meshgrid(torch.arange(h), torch.arange(w), indexing="ij")
flow = torch.stack(flow[::-1], dim=0).float()
flow[0] -= h/2
flow[1] -= w/2

img = flow_to_image(flow)
torch.save(img, "expected_flow.pt")
write_jpeg(img, "lol.jpg")  # if you want to look at it
```
![lol](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1190450/150966782-cbd3c2d5-6c4a-4efe-8f91-f14b55326840.jpg)


Then the test would compare the output of `img_to_tensor()` with the `expected_flow.pt` tensor that we would save to disk (and upload to the repo). It's only 32K so it's quite light, and I don't expect it to ever change.

Thanks for the updating! Overall I feel good about this! One concern about this one is that we still have coupling between workflow and step-function.

Right now, the remote function construction and serialization logic are in StepFunction, but the actual place where these are used is in Workflow.  We actually can put these into Workflow init function and **make the StepFunction only contains the logic about how to construct Workflow object** and make the Workflow contains the logic about how to execute it.

The reason is that, put the things close to the place where it's used can make the code cleaner and easier to understand and this reduce the dependence between two modules.

Think about this, if I read the code in workflow and want to understand that's happening there, I need to read the code in StepFunction to understand it. But if we put all the logic related to workflow there, then we don't need StepFunction to understand Workflow.

Do you think it make sense?

Updating the API docs for the Dask estimators is a bit complicated:

There are 3 lines like that in `dask.py`. We do this slicing of the doc so that the Dask estimators can still inherit most documentation language from the sklearn estimators.

cd docs/

@jameslamb @david-cortes @jmoralez  I like the new "type" argument. However, if I correctly understood the intended changes, they are in gross contrast what usual prediction functions involving link functions do. Sorry for this late input. I think it is important to consider it anyway.

According to `predict.glm()`, type "response" returns values on the scale of the response.  A Poisson regression with log link would return positive values, a binary logistic regression would return probabilities.

Type "link" means, on the other hand, that predictions are made in the link space. For a logistic regression, it would be the logit of the probabilities, for a Poisson regression the log expected counts etc. 

**LightGBM seems to use "link" in the exact opposite, i.e., in the sense that the inverse link is being applied. But this is what is usually be called "response"...**

My suggestions: 

- The default type is "response". Binary logistic will return probabilities. 
- There is either "raw" or "link" but not both. For historic reasons, I tend to "raw", but "link" is fine as well.
- There should be a type = "class" in order to get the majority class for those objectives where it makes sense. `glm.predict()`does not have this, but for instance "predict.glmnet()".
- Type "leaf" and "contrib" are okay.

Side note: `predict.glm()` uses `type = "link"` as the default, i.e., it predicts log odds instead of probabilities for a logistic regression. We should not do it similarly because then every logistic/Poisson/Gamma/Tweedie model would change its default.

- Object spill fails, no pull locations --> should retry object restore at the next tick.
- Object spill fails, all locations attempted --> should retry object restore at the next tick.
- OnLocationChange about a spilled URL and all pull locations empty while there are in-flight pulls --> should try object restore immediately

I'm a little worried about the robustness of the retry code overall. I'm not positive these cases are currently broken, but either way it's not great that there are so many possible corner cases. I got a little lost trying to figure out when the timer gets reset vs. not and what it means for the overall behavior if the timer is not reset.

I think it would be best to cut this PR down so it only guarantees "at most one pull request every retry interval" and ignore "don't retry a client until you've tried all the other clients" for now. Sorry, I know the majority of the PR is for the latter, but I'm not sure it's worth the complexity at the moment :/

LGTM, thanks @DanilBaibak! In other workflows that we don't typically run on a PR, we have this extra condition to run them in case the workflow is touched:

https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/f0a1df315db3c0fdec3d638895f6173809030bba/.github/workflows/tests-schedule.yml#L3-L8

So here this would be

```yml
on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - ".github/workflows/update-viablestrict.yml"
```

In addition, we only perform the actual action like reporting an issue or fast-forwarding the branch in case this happens on a schedule:

https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/f0a1df315db3c0fdec3d638895f6173809030bba/.github/workflows/tests-schedule.yml#L47-L49

With this setup you can avoid typo errors like this when working on the workflow, while not changing the actual functionality.

Something to note:

When a process forks we set `TelemetryWriter._forked = True` and prevent [appstarted](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-py/blob/v1.7.2/ddtrace/internal/telemetry/writer.py#L203), [appclosed](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-py/blob/v1.7.2/ddtrace/internal/telemetry/writer.py#L228) and [heartbeat](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-py/blob/v1.7.2/ddtrace/internal/telemetry/writer.py#L215) events from being queued ([link](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-py/blob/v1.7.2/ddtrace/internal/telemetry/writer.py#L57)).

Telemetry events are associated with runtime ids and runtime ids are unique to processes. Since the telemetry api has no way to link a child process to a parent process we try to send telemetry events only the master process. This was done to prevent "orphan" telemetry events from being sent to the backend. We will need to revisit this issue before implementing telemetry metrics.

Potential workaround: Use the runtime id of the master process to submit telemetry metrics in child processes. The telemetry team is aware of this issue and may have a solution (ex: Avoid using runtime id to identify application instances and introduce an "application_id" field). @mehulsonowal any thoughts on how we should approach this?

I definitely appreciate your point around this:
> I hope this MR can reconcile the two worlds: those who are dependent on `select/prefetch_related` and those who rely on custom `get_queryset`

It looks like overall this does the trick for that, and your approach of only using the custom resolver when `get_queryset` has been overridden seems fairly reasonable.

Hey @oke-aditya and sorry for the delay. Apart from your questions I've added two more comments inline.

> ```
> torchvision/models/quantization/utils.py:31: error: Incompatible types in assignment (expression has type "QConfig", variable has type "Union[Tensor, Module]")  [assignment]
>             model.qconfig = torch.quantization.QConfig(
>                             ^
> ```

The ignore is justified, because we are adding an attribute to a live object and `mypy` can't deal with that.

> ```
> torchvision/models/quantization/utils.py:35: error: "Tensor" not callable  [operator]
> model.fuse_model()
>         ^
> ```

`mypy` complains, because it correctly can't find a callable `fuse_model` parameter on an `nn.Module`. Thus, we need make the input type annotation more precise. I'm guessing we only accepting the quantized models that we have built-in? If yes, we could create a custom class mixin (for example `QuantizedModuleMixin`) that also inherits from `nn.Module` and adds this as abstract method. All our models could inherit from making `fuse_model` also available for checking. 

> The cat cat errors
> 
> ```
> torchvision/models/quantization/shufflenetv2.py:33: error: Argument 1 to "cat" of "FloatFunctional" has incompatible type "Tuple[Tensor, Any]"; expected "List[Tensor]"  [arg-type]
>                 out = self.cat.cat((x1, self.branch2(x2)), dim=1)
>                                     ^
> torchvision/models/quantization/shufflenetv2.py:35: error: Argument 1 to "cat" of "FloatFunctional" has incompatible type "Tuple[Any, Any]"; expected "List[Tensor]"  [arg-type]
>                 out = self.cat.cat((self.branch1(x), self.branch2(x)), dim=1)
>                                     ^
> ```

There are actually two errors here:

1. We are passing a tuple when a list is expected. We should be able to replace the parentheses with brackets: `(x1, self.branch2(x2))` to `[x1, self.branch2(x2)]`.
2. `self.branch*()` has no proper return type, so we need to cast ourselves: `self.branch2(x2)` to `cast(torch.Tensor, self.branch2(x2))`

> 
> The init errors, this probably has a nice workaround
> 
> ```
> torchvision/models/quantization/inception.py:111: error: "__init__" of "InceptionA" gets multiple values for keyword argument "conv_block"  [misc]
>             super(QuantizableInceptionA, self).__init__(conv_block=QuantizableBasicConv2d, *args, **kwargs)
>             ^
> 
> torchvision/models/quantization/inception.py:184: error: "__init__" of "InceptionAux" gets multiple values for keyword argument "conv_block"  [misc]
>             super(QuantizableInceptionAux, self).__init__(conv_block=QuantizableBasicConv2d, *args, **kwargs)
>             ^
> ```

I don't think so, because we currently make the hard assumption that no one passes `conv_block` as keyword argument. If someone would do, we would get an error at runtime, because we also pass it.

If we insist on using `**kwargs` here, I'm afraid there is no clean way to do it. One workaround would be to add a `conv_block: None = None` before the `**kwargs` and bail out if we encounter anything else than `None`. cc @fmassa @datumbox 

Note that there are a lot more of these in the [CI failure](https://app.circleci.com/pipelines/github/pytorch/vision/9701/workflows/c3ab1c23-934f-49dd-98bf-6d5991c01648/jobs/720421).

> Assigning a layer of model to None error
> 
> ```
> torchvision/models/quantization/googlenet.py:81: error: Incompatible types in assignment (expression has type "None", variable has type Module)  [assignment]
>                 model.aux1 = None
>                              ^
> ```

Porbably fine, because we do the same thing in the regular `GoogLeNet`. 

https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/db530d8c619a52753f4ce5207af7083162b4228e/torchvision/models/googlenet.py#L56-L57 

Not sure if this is a jit issue, but maybe we can type them as `Optional[nn.Module]` in the first place?

Could you please add a test in https://github.com/Yard1/LightGBM/blob/patch-1/tests/python_package_test/test_engine.py that ensures that this callback is pickleable? 

The primary purpose of this PR, as I understand it, is to make the early stopping callback pickleable so that it can be used by frameworks like Ray that are serializing the arguments to `lgb.train()` to send them around to different process. If I'm right about that, I think it would be valuable to explicitly test that so that future changes to LightGBM don't accidentally break that behavior.

Ideally this would work with `pickle`, `joblib`, and `cloudpickle`...could you try that? See the Dask tests for inspiration.
